main_summary.summary: Dr. Gwendolyn M. Patton, interviewed in Montgomery, Alabama on June 1, 2011 by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program, recounts her formation as a SNCC organizer and Tuskegee Institute Advancement League (TIAL) leader. Arriving at Tuskegee in fall 1961 from a Montgomery race-conscious family, she describes the May 1961 Freedom Rider violence at the Greyhound bus station, the all-night First Baptist Church standoff, and her aunts' divided response. She details a tuberculosis convalescence at the segregated Benson Sanatorium in Lafayette, Alabama (1963-1964), where she staged library and dietary protests. After returning to Tuskegee, she helped found TIAL and resisted SNCC's initial push to subsume the campus organization, citing Montgomery Improvement Association precedent. She organized the Tuskegee response to Bloody Sunday and the "march that wouldn't turn around" of March 1965, mobilizing approximately 1,500 students with James Forman. She discusses Lowndes County electoral organizing, Lucius Amerson's 1966 Macon County sheriff election, and her 1967 car accident that led to her refusal of Dr. Hume's amputation recommendation and transition to New York convalescence among medical-committee and Communist Party activists.
key_themes: Scholar-activist principle and Black college education
Distinction between integration, desegregation, and self-determination
Class divisions within Black Southern communities
main_summary.historical_significance:
Patton provides a first-person account of the Tuskegee Institute Advancement League's role in the Selma campaign and the East Black Belt organizing infrastructure that has been overshadowed by Selma and Montgomery in mainstream civil rights historiography. Her testimony documents the philosophical distinction between integration and self-determination that drove the Lowndes County Freedom Organization's electoral strategy, including the specific reasoning behind targeting sheriff, board of education, tax assessor, and coroner offices rather than state-level positions.

The interview preserves institutional details about Tuskegee under Dr. Luther H. Foster's presidency, the May 1961 Freedom Rider response in Montgomery, the segregated tuberculosis treatment system in Alabama, and the internal dynamics of SNCC's relationship with grassroots organizations. Patton's account of the disputed 1967 car accident and her refusal of amputation by Dr. Hume offers a rare personal counterpoint to standard movement narratives focused on public events.

chapters.title: Arriving at Tuskegee, Fall 1961
chapters.summary: Patton describes her arrival at Tuskegee Institute as a 17-year-old freshman, greeted by National Guard troops during the integration of Macon County schools. She recounts the campus architecture designed by Booker T. Washington with doors facing inward, and her family's grounding in Black history that prepared her to identify as a "race person" before college.

chapters.title: Robert Russa Moton and Tuskegee History
chapters.summary: Patton recounts the history of Robert Russa Moton's struggle over the Tuskegee VA hospital staffing in the 1920s, when Moton insisted on Black professional staff against Woodrow Wilson administration policy and Klan threats. She frames this history as the institutional foundation for her later activism and the 1967 paper she wrote in John Andrew Memorial Hospital.

chapters.title: Freedom Riders and the Mother's Day Massacre
chapters.summary: Patton describes the May 1961 Freedom Rider attack at the Greyhound bus station in Montgomery and the all-night mass meeting at First Baptist Church. She recounts her aunts Chick and Flora hosting Freedom Riders at their home, and Aunt Chick's instruction that no white person enter through the front door of her home while she still had to enter white homes through the back.

chapters.title: Benson Sanatorium, 1963-1964
chapters.summary: Patton describes her fourteen-month tuberculosis convalescence at the segregated Benson Sanatorium in Lafayette, Alabama. She recounts staging informal protests over library access for a nine-year-old Black patient, the food menu, and the use of the chapel and sitting room. She watched the March on Washington and the Birmingham church bombing on the ward's small television.

chapters.title: SNCC Arrives at Tuskegee, Fall 1964
chapters.summary: Patton describes returning to Tuskegee in fall 1964 and encountering SNCC organizers, including women who became tough mentors. She articulates her opposition to integration as an end in itself, rooted in her grandmother's 1956 instruction after the Montgomery Bus Boycott that the boycott had been about sitting anywhere she pleased, not about sitting next to white people. She analyzes the class dimension of Macon County school integration plans.

chapters.title: TIAL and the Scholar-Activist Principle
chapters.summary: Patton describes the founding of the Tuskegee Institute Advancement League (TIAL) and her refusal to let SNCC subsume the campus organization, citing the Montgomery Improvement Association's refusal to become an SCLC chapter. She recounts confrontations with Stokely Carmichael over college dropouts joining SNCC, and Bettina Aptheker's reaction to the campus ROTC program. She explains the scholar-activist principle and the practical reasons Tuskegee students needed ROTC scholarships.
related_events: SCLC formation at Holt Street Church 1957

chapters.title: The March That Wouldn't Turn Around
chapters.summary: Patton recounts the Tuskegee response to Bloody Sunday and Dr. King's turnaround at the Edmund Pettus Bridge, which she experienced as a broken promise. She describes mobilizing approximately 1,500 students via organized buses and a relay-march system that allowed students to walk to Montgomery without missing classes. She details the night at Dexter Avenue Baptist Church when deacons cut off heat, water, and lights, and her dispute with George Ware and James Forman over student agency.

chapters.title: Lowndes County and Electoral Self-Determination
chapters.summary: Patton describes the Lowndes County organizing strategy after the Voting Rights Act, including Tuskegee providing food for marchers. She articulates the Lowndes County Freedom Organization's decision to target sheriff, board of education, tax assessor, and coroner offices rather than state delegates or city government. She rejects the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party model and recounts the 1966 election of Lucius Amerson as Macon County sheriff against the consternation of the Black middle class.

chapters.title: 1967 Car Accident and New York Convalescence
chapters.summary: Patton recounts the February 10, 1967 car accident on the eve of the Tuskegee Black Power Conference, when she was driving a young man to her grandmother's home en route to Canada to avoid the draft. She describes refusing Dr. Hume's amputation recommendation at John Andrew Memorial Hospital and being retrieved by her paternal grandparents. Her transition to New York convalescence brought her into contact with Bettina Aptheker, Julia Wright, Mary Kochiyama, and Communist Party, Socialist Workers Party, and Progressive Labor Party organizers.
